steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Gayah claims that eliminating left turns at busy intersections improves overall traffic flow and reduces total travel times — even when drivers must take slightly longer routes. Supporting evidence must show that, after eliminating left turns, people can actually get more done or travel more efficiently overall, not just that individual turns are faster or slower.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — drivers reach more addresses daily after elimination: a delivery company reports higher daily delivery counts after the city eliminated left turns. More addresses reached per day means drivers are moving through the city faster overall — exactly what Gayah's claim predicts. ✓
  • Choice B — survey satisfaction after installing left-turn signals: this tests adding left-turn signals, not eliminating left turns. It addresses the opposite intervention and says nothing about whether elimination improves efficiency. ✗
  • Choice C — drivers wait longer at intersections without left-turn signals: this only measures wait time at individual left-turn intersections, not overall travel efficiency. It also doesn't involve eliminating left turns at all. ✗
  • Choice D — school buses took two minutes longer after elimination: longer route times directly contradict Gayah's claim that travel times improve. This would weaken, not support, the claim. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
